title: a honeypot cache that watches and learns
blurb: single binary, ~2k LOC. how the cache decides what is worth keeping, and what that tells you about who is knocking.
body:
The cache started as a joke. I wanted to know who was scraping an endpoint, and a cache sits in exactly the right place to find out.

## the first three designs were wrong

1. LRU with a twist — evict the least recently used, but never the suspicious ones. This kept garbage forever.
2. LFU — frequency is easy to game with a tight loop.
3. A scoring function nobody could explain.

```go
func (c *Cache) shouldKeep(e *entry) bool {
  if e.hits < 2 {
    return false
  }
  return !e.sourceLooksLikeEnumeration()
}
```

> A cache that forgets on a timer tells you nothing. A cache that forgets on *evidence* tells you who your enemies are.

The final design keeps a 64-byte rolling profile per client. That is the whole trick.

## what I would change

Next time I would make the profile pluggable from the start. Hard-coding the heuristic was fast to ship and slow to evolve.

title: reading kyber without hand-waving
blurb: post-quantum KEMs are not magic. a slow walk through ring-LWE for people who can read code but not papers.
body:
Every explanation of Kyber I found was either three equations or three hundred pages. I wanted the version in between.

## the one idea

Lattice crypto hides a secret in *noise*. You publish something close to a lattice point; only the holder of the secret can round back to the exact point.

```
public  = A·s + e      // e is small noise
secret  = s
```

That `e` is the entire game. Too little noise and it's not secure; too much and you can't decrypt.

## where people lose the thread

The modular reductions. Keep a number line in your head and most of the mystery evaporates.

title: metering for the highlights, letting the shadows fall
blurb: a short note on exposure discipline for night and neon — and why I stopped trusting the histogram's left side.
body:
Neon lies to your meter. The camera sees a dark frame and tries to lift it; you end up with grey mush and blown signage.

## the rule

Meter for the brightest thing you care about. Let everything else go dark.

> The shadows are not a problem to be solved. They are most of the photograph.

In Tokyo this meant shooting two stops under what the camera begged for. The Golden Gai frames in the [photo](./photo) page are all metered this way.

title: 600 lines of tracing
blurb: what you actually need from a distributed tracer, and how little code it takes once you stop trying to support everything.
body:
Tracing has an enormous surface area and a tiny core. The core is: a trace id, a span, a parent pointer, and a clock.

```go
type Span struct {
  TraceID  uint64
  SpanID   uint64
  ParentID uint64
  Start    time.Time
}
```

## the part everyone over-builds

Transport. You do not need a collector on day one. A ring buffer and a `/debug/trace` endpoint will carry you further than you think.

title: notes on keeping a single binary
blurb: a running list of the small disciplines that keep a project shippable as one static file.
body:
A loose collection, updated when I learn something.

- Embed assets; do not read from disk at runtime unless the user asked.
- One config struct, one place it is parsed.
- Logs are an interface, not a `fmt.Println`.

> Every dependency is a promise someone else has to keep.

title: why the blog is just markdown
blurb: the case for a boring content pipeline, and the one place I let myself be fancy.
body:
The body you are reading is a markdown string from an API. That is the whole pipeline.

## the fancy part

The *rendering* is where the terminal theme earns its keep — line gutters, fenced code with a comment color, a reading-progress bar. But the source is plain text, and that is on purpose.

title: what iceland taught me about shooting wide
blurb: wide lenses promise everything and deliver nothing unless you give the frame a job.
body:
A 16mm lens will happily include the whole valley and make all of it boring.

## give the frame a job

Every wide shot needs one near thing and one far thing. The near thing is the assignment; the far thing is the reward.

> Vastness only reads as vast when something small is standing next to it.
